one_line_takeaway: A conceptual, non-security taxonomy paper proposing a 6-level 'BASE Scale' (analogous to SAE J3016) plus a latency/information-theoretic formalism and NLL-based liability rule for benchmarking and safety-casing autonomous experiment agents at large-scale scientific user facilities -- it contains no adversarial threat model and should not be tagged as AI-security content.
key_finding: Applying the BASE scale to five existing published autonomous-experiment systems (Table 2), the paper finds that the majority operate at Level 2 (Reflexive): Adaptive XRD (Szymanski et al.) and Sparse Scanning (Yager et al.) are classified Level 2 because they optimize sampling/signal-to-noise reactively without a temporal digital twin predicting future states; Gaussian Process MAPs (Maffettone et al.) are classified 'Level 2+' because they minimize posterior uncertainty over a static parameter space but lack Level-3 anomaly-detection logic. Only two systems reach Level 3 (Heuristic): ANDiE (McDannald et al.), which uses Weiss/Ising physics priors to hunt for a magnetic phase transition while avoiding hysteresis, and the SNS Edge-to-Exascale system (Yin et al.), which uses a Temporal Fusion Transformer to forecast neutron scattering pattern evolution and acts on predicted future state. From this the paper concludes that true semantic (Level 3) autonomy is rare in current deployed systems and requires first resolving the Level-2/3 Inference Barrier. For the illustrative cathode case study, the paper cites (from the companion topography paper, ref. 5) that stress-concentrator notch tips generate localized stresses of approximately 85 MPa against a material yield strength of 39 MPa (a factor of ~2 above yield), used to argue that failure is governed by rare, deterministic geometric features rather than being a stochastic bulk phenomenon -- and from this the paper argues (not measures) that a Level-1 grid scan wastes over 99% of available beam flux on the elastic bulk, versus a Level-3 agent that concentrates flux on the failure-relevant kurtosis outliers.
core_contribution: The paper proposes the BASE (Benchmarking Autonomy in Scientific Experiments) Scale, a 6-level taxonomy (Levels 0-5) adapted from the automotive SAE J3016 standard for classifying autonomy in experiments run at Large-Scale User Facilities (synchrotrons, neutron sources, etc.). Unlike prior owner-operator taxonomies (e.g. for electron microscopy), BASE is built around the 'transient user model' where beamtime is limited to 48-96 hours, so agents must be capable of zero-shot deployment via Sim-to-Real transfer from physics-based Digital Twins rather than on-instrument reinforcement learning. The author defines two structural discontinuities in the taxonomy: the 'Inference Barrier' (Level 2->3), the latency threshold at which raw detector data (sinograms, diffractograms) must be inverted into physical space before a control decision is meaningful, and the 'Liability Threshold' (Level 3->4), where risk ownership shifts from the human operator to the agent's validation framework once an agent gets active hardware control. The paper formalizes a latency-budget control criterion (tau_loop = tau_readout + tau_transport + (tau_reduce + tau_inference) + tau_actuation <= t_dynamics/kappa, with safety factor kappa >= 10), defines an information-theoretic control objective ('Entropy-Scaled Measurement Efficiency', ESME) as a constrained optimization (Heuristic Policy Function, Eq. 3) subject to an ODD (Operational Design Domain) safety constraint and the observability/latency constraint, and proposes a Sim-to-Real 'certification' scheme in which a Negative Log-Likelihood-based Sim-to-Real Gap (Delta_gap = -ln P_sim(s'_obs | s_t, a_t)) is compared to a calibrated validity bound epsilon_valid to allocate liability between the facility (if Delta_gap > epsilon_valid, i.e. the outcome was physically unpredicted by the digital twin) and the user (if Delta_gap <= epsilon_valid, i.e. the agent ignored a correctly predicted risk). The taxonomy is then applied to classify five existing autonomous-experiment systems from the literature (Table 2) as a demonstration of the scale's discriminative utility, and a high-nickel battery cathode failure case study is used to illustrate a theoretical efficiency argument for Level 3 vs Level 1 scanning.
threat_model: None. This is not an adversarial-security paper: there is no attacker, no adversarial input, no model-extraction/poisoning/jailbreak scenario, and no evaluation against an adversary. The paper's 'risk' framing is about operational/physical safety and liability apportionment for autonomous lab agents that could physically damage hardware or samples through erroneous (non-adversarial) actions -- e.g. an agent driving a motor beyond safe limits, causing beam loss, or acting confidently outside its training distribution ('confident hallucinations'). This is closer to functional safety / reliability engineering (analogous to automotive ODD/SAE J3016 safety cases) than to AI/information security. No threat actor, attack surface, or security control (authentication, access control against malicious actors, confidentiality/integrity/availability of data) is discussed.
limitations: This is a single-author, no-new-experiments position/framework paper: the taxonomy, the ESME metric, and the latency-budget formalism are proposed and argued for, but not empirically validated against real facility deployments by this paper -- the ESME metric's full derivation is explicitly deferred to a companion paper (ref. 4, Lu et al. 2025/2026) rather than being derived here. The Table 2 classification of five prior systems is a qualitative mapping performed by the author based on published descriptions, with no inter-rater reliability check or the original authors' input on whether the BASE-level assignment matches their own system's intent. The central case study (high-nickel cathode notches) draws its numeric claims (85 MPa vs 39 MPa yield, >90%/>99% flux waste) from the author's own separate preprint/case study rather than from experiments conducted in this paper, and the 'order-of-magnitude efficiency gain' for Level 3 is described as a theoretical estimate rather than a measured result. The liability-allocation formalism (Eq. 6, using NLL-based Sim-to-Real Gap vs. a calibrated epsilon_valid) is proposed but not tested against any real incident data or a worked numerical example. The paper also acknowledges as an open problem (the 'Exploration Paradox') that purely entropy-driven agents can be attracted to non-physical high-entropy noise (e.g. detector glitches, beam dumps) rather than genuine phenomena, and only sketches a mitigating indicator-function condition (Eq. 7) without demonstrating it in practice.

quantitative_results: Illustrative cathode case study (from cited companion work, ref. 5): stress concentrators at notch tips generate localized stresses of approximately 85 MPa, exceeding the material's 39 MPa yield strength by a factor of ~2.

Argued (theoretical, not measured in this paper) that a standard Level-1 grid scan wastes over 99% of available beam flux measuring the stable bulk of a material (stated twice in the text, once as '>99%' and once, in the Figure 2 caption, as '>90%' of beamtime).

Worked latency-budget numeric example: a 100 MB detector frame on a 10 GbE link introduces an irreducible transport delay of approximately 80 ms; capturing a crack propagating at 1 micron/s at 100 nm spatial resolution yields a characteristic process timescale t_dynamics of approximately 100 ms, which under the safety factor kappa=10 requires total loop latency tau_loop < 10 ms (i.e. a control loop frequency exceeding 100 Hz).
baselines_compared: Adaptive XRD (Szymanski et al., npj Comput. Mater. 2023) -- classified BASE Level 2 (Reflexive)
Sparse Scanning (Yager et al., Nanotechnology 2023) -- classified BASE Level 2 (Reflexive)
ANDiE (McDannald et al., Appl. Phys. Rev. 2022) -- classified BASE Level 3 (Heuristic)
Edge-to-Exascale / SNS neutron scattering (Yin et al., Struct. Dyn. 2024) -- classified BASE Level 3 (Heuristic)
Gaussian Process MAPs (Maffettone et al., arXiv 2301.09177) -- classified BASE Level 2+ (Reflexive)
